exemplar.title: To what extent do Qatar's green energy efficiency policies, as part of the Qatar National Vision 2030, influence the employment rate and competitiveness of firms in the construction industry?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do Qatar's green energy efficiency policies, as part of the Qatar National Vision 2030, influence the employment rate and competitiveness of firms in the construction industry?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The investigation topic is communicated accurately and consistently, with an appropriately narrow economic focus on Qatarâs green-energy policies and the construction sector.
The research question is clearly stated and used effectively to signpost the structure of the essay.
There is clear evidence of informed source selection, including labour-force statistics, official reports and a primary survey, demonstrating methodological appropriateness.
weaknesses: The research question bundles two distinct outcomes (employment and competitiveness), which occasionally weakens focus and alignment of the analysis.
Methodology description omits key details (sampling frame, response rate, data-cleaning procedures) and lacks justification for the chosen time period.
Occasional digressions into general sustainability context detract slightly from the economic focus of the investigation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a clear and appropriate topic focus with an effectively communicated purpose, meriting placement in the mid-range of the 5â6 band. However, the broad research question and incomplete methodology details introduce limitations, justifying a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Economic theory (AD/AS, externalities, subsidies, labour mobility) is applied to the Qatari context with clear understanding of core concepts.
Specialised terminology is used accurately and consistently, supporting the development of arguments.
Relevant source material (official statistics, sustainability reports) is integrated into the discussion to underpin key claims.
weaknesses: Occasional conceptual errors (e.g., mislabelling productivity gain as a negative externality) reduce coherence of the analysis.
Overreliance on non-academic sources (ChatGPT, corporate blogs) undermines the rigour and appropriateness of some citations.
Some assertions (e.g., link between expatriate hiring and green policies) lack sufficient evidence and would benefit from additional support.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates sound knowledge of economic concepts and generally appropriate application of source material, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band. However, conceptual misinterpretations and questionable source choices temper the overall performance, warranting a score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: The investigationâs research is largely appropriate and relevant, drawing on official statistics, government strategy documents and primary survey data.
Analysis is clear and conclusions are mostly supported by quantitative calculations (percentage changes) and graphical tools.
The discussion offers a reasoned argument, synthesising stakeholder impacts and policy trade-offs effectively.
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven: the essay lacks robustness checks (e.g., statistical significance, error margins) and deeper econometric or time-series evaluation.
Critical evaluation is partial, with limited discussion of counter-evidence (e.g., non-Qatari employment dips, âNoâ survey responses).
Some policy evaluations (e.g., subsidy costs) are descriptive rather than quantitatively supported, reducing overall critical depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work delivers consistent, appropriate research and logical analysis with supported conclusions, fitting the middle of the 7â9 band (4â6 band for EE). Given the uneven depth and only partial critical evaluation, a score of 5 out of 12 reflects a strong but not fully sophisticated critical-thinking performance.

strengths: The report follows IB structural conventions (title page, contents page, logical headings) and is fully paginated, aiding navigation.
Tables of contents and appendices are well organised and clearly labelled, supporting transparency of research instruments.
weaknesses: Formatting inconsistencies (mis-aligned tables, low-resolution diagrams without captions, mixed font sizes, stray inline LaTeX) detract from overall polish.
Figure captions and bibliography entries lack consistent IB-style formatting, and bilingual headers are sometimes unclear.
Some content is redundant or misplaced (e.g., strengths subsection in conclusion, Personal Code header in the introduction), disrupting flow and conciseness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the structure meets IB conventions, significant layout and formatting issues hinder readability and professional presentation. This aligns with the lower end of the 1â2 band, justifying a score of 2 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is quoted verbatim and used as an organisational signpost; however, it bundles two distinct outcomes (employment rate and competitiveness) which occasionally pulls analysis in divergent directions. While the wording is clear, the breadth weakens precision and full alignment with subsequent discussion. The question therefore meets the descriptors for a clear but only partially focused RQ.

The topicâQatarâs green-energy policies and their impact on construction employment and competitivenessâis stated explicitly in the title, reiterated in the introduction and consistently referenced throughout the study. The scope (Qatar, 2018-23, construction sector, Vision 2030) is appropriately narrow for a 4 000-word EE and conveys a clear economic focus. Occasional side-trips into general sustainability issues do not seriously detract from the overall clarity. Consequently, the communication is accurate and the purpose and focus are appropriate, supporting placement in the 5-6 band.

Most evidence (LFS tables, official sustainability reports) is relevant and woven into argument, but reliance on ChatGPT outputs and corporate-finance blogs dilutes rigour and occasionally produces thin linkage to claims (e.g. productivity as negative externality). Application is therefore mostlyâthough not alwaysâappropriate, placing it solidly in the 3-4 band.

The report follows IB conventionsâtitle page, contents, introduction, body, conclusion, bibliographyâand the contents page aids navigation. Headings are logical and pagination is correct, fulfilling the descriptor for a clearly appropriate structure.

Basic layout elementsâword count, title, page numbersâare present, but formatting inconsistencies (mis-aligned tables, low-resolution diagrams without proper captions, mixed font sizes, stray inline LaTeX) detract from polish. These issues indicate that some layout considerations are missing or incorrectly applied.

Arguments are logically structured and the conclusion synthesises stakeholder impacts and policy trade-offs. Evaluation is present (e.g., fiscal dependence on hydrocarbon revenue, survey limitations) but depth is uneven and counter-evidence limited. This represents reasoned argument with partial critical evaluation, appropriate for the upper segment of the 4-6 band.

The essay applies percentage-change calculations and standard diagrams to generate conclusions that are generally supported, yet analysis is predominantly qualitative and lacks deeper econometric or time-series treatment. Assertions are sometimes accepted without robustness checks. This aligns with clearâbut not consistently sophisticatedâanalysis characteristic of the middle-upper part of the 4-6 band.

The student demonstrates sound understanding of core economic ideasâAD/AS, externalities, subsidies, labour mobilityâand can relate them to Qatarâs context. Weaknesses arise in occasional conceptual errors (labelling productivity gain as negative externality) and over-generalised assertions. Knowledge is therefore clear but not consistently authoritative.

The bulk of evidence stems from official statistics, government strategy documents and industry reports that are clearly germane to the research question. Although some non-academic sources are present, the majority of material remains appropriate and relevant, fulfilling the top of the 4-6 band.

The student draws upon labour-force statistics, QatarEnergy reports and a primary survey, demonstrating some informed source choice. Yet the methodology description is cursory: sampling frame, survey distribution, response rate and data-cleaning procedures are omitted; no justification is offered for time-period selection beyond convenience. The range is adequate but not fully appropriate or transparently chosen, meriting the middle of the 3-4 band.

Specialised terms such as âallocative efficiencyâ, ârecessionary gapâ, âQatarizationâ and âmarginal social costâ are deployed accurately and consistently, with only minor lapses. Terminology use supports argumentation and reflects a competent command, justifying the upper end of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The application of Green Growth Theory provides a coherent theoretical lens for evaluating long-term sustainability impacts.
The conclusion synthesizes employment, GDP, and subsidy insights effectively, providing a cohesive summary of findings.
The âPersonal Codeâ header appears within the introduction rather than only on the title page, disrupting flow; relocate for consistency.
Citing ChatGPT as a source undermines academic rigor; replace generative AI references with peer-reviewed or authoritative publications.
Diagram 3 effectively demonstrates the impact of expansionary fiscal policy on AD and SRAS, linking theory to the Qatari context.
The student correctly provides the word count at the start (3930/4000 words), satisfying the formatting requirement.
The research question is clearly stated and framed, providing appropriate focus for the investigation.
The narrative overlooks the 2021 dip in non-Qatari employment; analyzing these fluctuations would deepen the discussion of policy effects.
The 19% of âNoâ responses are not discussed; exploring their reasons would provide a more balanced critical evaluation.
The analysis of government subsidies and stakeholder impacts offers a reasoned evaluation of policy trade-offs.
The title is prominently displayed at the beginning of the report, meeting the formatting requirement for a clear investigation title.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well-organised, effectively guiding the reader through the reportâs structure.
Appendices are listed in the contents but will not be assessed; ensure the main text remains the primary focus of evaluation.
Diagram 1 correctly labels MPC and MSC curves, reflecting accurate application of externality theory.
The personal anecdote about attending the World Cup adds context but detracts from the economic focus; consider streamlining to emphasize economic rationale.
The student effectively links QNV 2030 targets to specific environmental and economic goals, demonstrating clear contextual understanding.
The percent-change calculation is presented clearly using LaTeX, demonstrating rigorous quantitative analysis skills.
The interpretation of a 3.4% increase omits any discussion of statistical significance or error margins; consider including robustness checks.
The market structure discussion ends mid-sentence (âThe market structureâ); the student should complete and clarify this concept for coherence.
Restating the research question verbatim helps signposting, but bundling multiple outcomes weakens focus; consider narrowing to one key variable.
Methodology describes data sources but omits sampling frame, response rate, and cleaning procedures; include these details to strengthen transparency.
The articulation of graphs and economic concepts demonstrates appropriate application of micro and macro theories in methodology.
The choice of the 2018â2023 period goes unexplained; student should justify this timeline as the most relevant window for QNV 2030 impact.
Table 1 integrates official labour-force statistics effectively, underpinning the analysis of expatriate employment trends.
Discussion of subsidy limitations lacks quantitative estimates of fiscal impact; include approximate subsidy costs or budget figures for precision.
Fiscal policy limitations could be illustrated with real examples of subsidy adjustments in comparable economies to reinforce the critique.
The limitations section candidly acknowledges sampling bias in the survey, demonstrating reflective evaluation of methodology.
The strengths subsection is overly descriptive and lacks clear topic sentences; tighten prose to enhance focus and readability.
Column headers include untranslated Arabic labels without English equivalents, which may confuse readers; ensure bilingual clarity.
The inclusion of primary survey data in Diagram 2 enriches the analysis with stakeholder perspectives and empirical insights.
The correlation between expatriate hiring and green policies is asserted without direct evidence; support with additional data or citations.
Classifying productivity gains as a negative production externality misinterprets economic concepts; revise to accurately distinguish external costs.
Figure captions lack consistent formatting (e.g., italicized source and numbered figure), which should follow IB conventions.
Diagram 7 clearly illustrates the effect of a government subsidy on supply and price, linking graphical analysis to policy discussion.
The explanation of subsidy effects repeats earlier arguments; reduce redundancy to maintain conciseness and focus.
Introducing market consolidation risks in the conclusion without prior discussion creates new content; reserve novel points for the body of the essay.
Qualitative discussion of GDP impacts lacks supporting quantitative estimates; incorporating econometric or time-series data would deepen the analysis.
Highlighting that 81% see competitiveness gains from sustainability (Diagram 6) reinforces the argument with strong empirical support.
Articulating key assumptions (e.g., continuation of subsidies) demonstrates reflective critical thinking in methodological evaluation.
The bibliography is split across pages and contains duplicate entries; consolidate into a single, consistently formatted list.
The explanation of recessionary gap in Diagram 4 demonstrates accurate use of macroeconomic theory in policy evaluation.
Reliance on ChatGPT to summarize policy timing should be replaced with citations from official government or peer-reviewed sources to enhance credibility.
The survey appendices are well-organized and clearly labeled, facilitating transparency of research instruments.
Diagram 5 presents survey percentages but lacks deeper interpretation of why cost savings and unemployment reduction were prioritized by respondents.
Multiple footnotes cite ChatGPT; replace all AI tool references with credible academic or industry sources to uphold rigour.
Listing ChatGPT and Grammarly as sources undermines academic credibility; remove AI tools and reference only primary and peer-reviewed materials.
